1: ἐπὶ τῆς θαλάσσης τῆς Τιβεριάδος -- the Sea of Tiberias (= Sea of Galilee, named for the emperor). The return to Galilee recalls the setting of the earliest calls to discipleship (John 1:43). The chapter is widely regarded as an epilogue added to the original Gospel (which ends with John 20:30-31), but internal vocabulary and style are consistent with the rest of the Gospel. The phrase ephanerōsen palin -- "appeared again" -- signals a third manifestation; the Evangelist counts appearances (cf. John 21:14).
2: Seven disciples are named. The number seven has symbolic weight in the Gospel (seven signs, seven "I am" sayings) and may not be incidental. Thomas and Nathanael appear together, as at key moments (John 1:45-51; John 11:16). The Zebedee brothers are named only here in the Fourth Gospel -- John is never named directly in his own Gospel.
3: ὑπάγω ἁλιεύειν -- "I am going fishing." Peter's statement is characteristically decisive. Whether this represents a return to the pre-call life or simply practical activity during the interval is debated. The Evangelist does not editorialize. ἐν ἐκείνῃ τῇ νυκτὶ ἐπίασαν οὐδέν -- "that night they caught nothing" -- prepares for the abundance that the risen Jesus provides.
4: οὐ μέντοι ᾔδεισαν οἱ μαθηταὶ ὅτι Ἰησοῦς ἐστιν -- "the disciples did not know it was Jesus." Non-recognition is a pattern in the resurrection appearances (John 20:14; Luke 24:16). The Evangelist again notes the non-recognition without explaining it. The physical setting -- shore at dawn, distance across the water -- may account for it practically.
5: Παιδία -- "Children" (or "lads") -- a vocative address that appears nowhere else in the Gospel proper. It is a familiar term of address; the disciples do not yet recognize the speaker. The question about food (prosphagion, a relish or condiment eaten with bread) sets up the contrast with the miraculous provision to follow.
6: εἰς τὰ δεξιά -- "on the right side" -- the miracle is the identification signal that triggers recognition in John 21:7. The parallel with the miraculous catch in Luke 5:1-11 is structurally close but distinct in context: that was the call to discipleship; this is restoration after the crucifixion.
7: ὁ κύριός ἐστιν -- "It is the Lord!" The Beloved Disciple is again the first to perceive who Jesus is (cf. John 20:8). Peter's response -- wrapping his outer garment around him (for he was stripped for work) and jumping into the sea -- is impulsive action rather than reflection. The parenthetical explanation of his nakedness (ēn gar gymnos) is the kind of detail the Evangelist attributes to eyewitness memory.
8: Brief connector. The other disciples come in the boat, dragging the net. The Evangelist notes the distance -- about two hundred cubits (roughly a hundred yards) -- with the precision of a source who was present.
9: ἀνθρακιάν -- "a fire of coals" (anthrakian). The word occurs only twice in the NT -- here and at John 18:18, where Peter stood warming himself at a coal fire in the high priest's courtyard when he denied Jesus. The repetition is almost certainly deliberate: the scene of Peter's restoration mirrors the scene of Peter's denial, with the coal fire as the common detail.
10: Jesus calls for fish from the catch the disciples have just made -- not only from what he has already prepared. The invitation to bring their fish and add it to his provision mirrors the feeding miracle logic (John 6:9-11) where Jesus takes what is offered.
11: ἑκατὸν πεντήκοντα τρία -- 153 fish. Several explanations have been proposed: Jerome noted that ancient naturalists counted 153 species of fish, symbolizing the universality of the mission; mathematically, 153 is the triangular number of 17 (sum of 1 through 17), and 17 = 10 + 7 (completeness). None of these are excluded, but the Evangelist's primary interest may be in the precision itself -- the count is what you remember when you were there and counted. The unbroken net despite the number is noted without explanation.
12: οὐδεὶς ἐτόλμα τῶν μαθητῶν ἐξετάσαι αὐτόν -- "none of the disciples dared ask him, Who are you?" They knew it was the Lord. The combination of knowing and not asking creates a charged, awed atmosphere. The recognition is certain but the encounter is not ordinary -- there is a quality to the risen Jesus that makes the question superfluous and also somehow not entirely possible.
13: ἔρχεται Ἰησοῦς καὶ λαμβάνει τὸν ἄρτον καὶ δίδωσιν αὐτοῖς -- "Jesus comes and takes the bread and gives it to them" -- the same verb sequence as 6:11 at the feeding of the five thousand. The eucharistic resonance is available without the Evangelist making it explicit; the vocabulary of taking, giving, and distributing recalls both the feeding miracle and the Last Supper.
14: τρίτον ἐφανερώθη Ἰησοῦς -- "the third time Jesus was manifested" -- the Evangelist counts appearances. The passive ephanerōthē (was manifested) places the initiative with Jesus; the disciples receive manifestation rather than discovering him. The counting underlines that the resurrection is a series of historical encounters, not an enduring visionary state.
15: ἀγαπᾷς με πλέον τούτων -- "do you love me more than these?" The comparative pleon toutōn recalls Peter's boast (John 13:37: "I will lay down my life for you") and his implied superiority over the other disciples. Jesus uses agapas (agapaō, G25); Peter replies with philō (phileō, G5368). The glossary entries preserve the traditional distinction -- agapaō as deliberate, principled love; phileō as warm personal affection -- though many scholars argue John uses the terms synonymously elsewhere. What the Evangelist preserves is that Jesus asks one kind of love and Peter answers with another. Commission: boske ta arnia mou -- "feed my lambs" (boskein, tend/pasture; arnia, young lambs).
16: The second exchange. Jesus repeats with agapas but drops "more than these" -- the comparison is no longer raised. Peter replies again with philō. The commission shifts: poimaine ta probata mou -- "shepherd my sheep" (poimainein, shepherd/govern; probata, sheep). The verb changes from boskein (feed/tend) to poimainein (shepherd/govern) -- the pastoral image broadens from feeding to leadership.
17:
λέγει αὐτῷ τὸ τρίτον Φιλεῖς με -- "he says to him the third time, Do you love me?" On the third asking, Jesus shifts to Peter's own word -- Phileis. The change explains Peter's pain: Jesus seemed now to be doubting the personal affection Peter had claimed. The Evangelist records this emotional detail (elupēthē ho Petros, Peter was grieved) without sentimentalizing it.

The three-fold commissioning explicitly restores Peter after the three-fold denial (John 18:17, 25, 27). The parallel is structural -- the Evangelist builds it into the scene deliberately. The final commission returns to the verb boskein (boske ta probata mou), forming a bracket with John 21:15.
18: ἐκτενεῖς τὰς χεῖράς σου -- "you will stretch out your hands" -- the phrase is used in Greek for the outstretched arms of crucifixion. The Evangelist's editorial note in John 21:19 confirms this reading. The contrast with youth (neōteros, when you girded yourself and walked where you wished) and old age (when another will gird you and lead you where you do not wish) describes the loss of self-determination. The three pairs of clauses form an antithetical structure of freedom and its surrender.
19: ποίῳ θανάτῳ δοξάσει τὸν θεόν -- "by what kind of death he would glorify God." The Evangelist's interpretive note uses the same formula as 12:33 and John 18:32 (poiō thanatō -- what kind of death). The formula signals a predictive speech act that has been or will be fulfilled. Peter's death glorifies God -- not merely submits to God, but actively glorifies, as Jesus's death glorified the Father (John 17:1). The command Akolouthei moi -- "Follow me" -- carries the full freight of what following means in John 12:25-26.
20: τὸν μαθητὴν ὃν ἠγάπα ὁ Ἰησοῦς -- the designation of the Beloved Disciple is introduced again with a detail that ties him to the Last Supper (en tō deinpō, at the supper; anepesen epi to stēthos autou, leaned on his chest, John 13:23-25). The Evangelist locates the Beloved Disciple in this climactic scene with precision -- already following, already there. Peter asks about him.
21: οὗτος δὲ τί -- "but what about this man?" Peter's question is natural after receiving a personal prediction of martyrdom. Jesus's answer deflects it entirely. The Evangelist uses the scene to draw a distinction between Peter's calling (follow; the call to martyrdom) and the Beloved Disciple's (bearing witness, John 21:24).
22: ἐὰν αὐτὸν θέλω μένειν ἕως ἔρχομαι -- "if I want him to remain until I come, what is that to you?" The conditional ean with subjunctive expresses a hypothetical: Jesus does not assert that the Beloved Disciple will remain. The emphasis falls on the final words to Peter: su moi akolouthei -- "you, follow me." Peter's calling is unaffected by another disciple's different calling. The personal address (su moi) isolates Peter -- this is his commission, regardless of another's.
23: οὐκ εἶπεν δὲ αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς ὅτι οὐκ ἀποθνῄσκει -- "Jesus did not say that he would not die." The Evangelist corrects a misreading that had circulated in the community -- the rumor that the Beloved Disciple would not die before the Parousia. The clarification is careful: Jesus said only a conditional ("if I want him to remain"), which is not a promise of longevity. The correction is best explained by the death of the Beloved Disciple -- or his approaching death -- making the rumor a live issue in the community.
24: ὁ γράψας ταῦτα -- "the one who wrote these things." The Evangelist identifies the Beloved Disciple as the author of the Gospel (ho martyrei... ho grapsas, the one who testifies... who wrote). The "we know" (oidamen) that follows is a communal certification, distinct from the Beloved Disciple's first-person authority. The community attests the truth of his witness. This double layer of testimony (author's and community's) functions like a colophon.
25: οἶμαι δὲ οὐδὲ αὐτὸν τὸν κόσμον χωρῆσαι τὰ γραφόμενα βιβλία -- "I suppose the world itself would not contain the books that would be written." The Evangelist closes with a first-person singular (oimai, I suppose) -- the only occurrence in the Gospel, strengthening the case that the Beloved Disciple is speaking directly. The hyperbole is a rhetorical adynaton, asserting the inexhaustibility of what Jesus did and said. A similar device closes Ecclesiastes 12:12. The Evangelist ends not with closure but with abundance -- more than can be contained.
